claim: UNESCO's 2024 Global Report on Teachers estimates a global shortage of 44 million teachers for universal primary and secondary education by 2030, with Sub-Saharan Africa facing the most severe shortages and pupil-to-trained-teacher ratios exceeding 200:1 in some districts.
method: Reviewed UNESCO teacher report for global shortage estimates, regional breakdown, and deployment-equity analysis.
limitations: Teacher-qualification data varies by country definition — what counts as 'trained' differs across national education systems.
The 44 million figure is a modeled projection based on enrollment targets, not an observed shortage count.
Deployment data aggregates national statistics — sub-national distribution within countries is not systematically reported.
Teacher-absence data is not captured in the shortage estimate — effective teaching capacity is lower than teacher counts suggest.

claim: Service Delivery Indicator surveys in multiple African countries document that teacher-content knowledge and pedagogical-skill scores vary dramatically within countries, with rural schools scoring 20-40 percent lower than urban schools on standardized teacher assessments.

source.title: Service Delivery Indicator Surveys — World Bank

method: Reviewed SDI survey methodology, teacher-assessment instruments, and rural-urban comparison results across multiple African countries.
limitations: SDI surveys are conducted in a limited number of African countries — coverage outside Africa is minimal.
Teacher-assessment scores are measured at a point in time — they do not capture whether rural teachers improve with experience or support.
The 20-40 percent rural-urban gap is a range across countries — specific country gaps may be larger or smaller.
SDI surveys measure teacher knowledge and skill, not actual classroom teaching effectiveness — the relationship between assessment scores and student learning is imperfect.

claim: National EMIS data provides school-level teacher counts and qualification data for some countries, but data quality, update frequency, and public accessibility vary enormously — many countries do not publish sub-national teacher-distribution data, making cross-country deployment-equity comparison methodologically challenging.
method: Reviewed EMIS documentation, data-quality assessments, and public-availability status across low-income countries.
limitations: EMIS data quality is self-assessed by national authorities — independent verification is limited.
Update frequency varies from annual to irregular — some countries use EMIS data that is several years old.
Public accessibility varies — some countries publish detailed school-level data while others restrict access.
Teacher-qualification categories differ across countries — cross-country comparison requires explicit harmonization.
URL changed from the UNESCO EMIS page (404 as of 2026-06-28) to the UNESCO Education Sector page; EMIS content may have been reorganized.
